title: Apneic Infant in Waiting Room
hpi: 3-month-old male brought back from the waiting room by a parent after staff noticed he was limp and unresponsive. Mom reports he was seen in clinic 3 days ago for upper respiratory symptoms. In the waiting room he had an episode where he turned blue and stopped breathing for approximately 30 seconds, then took a few gasping breaths. He is now minimally responsive.
exam: Limp infant with agonal gasping respirations. Central cyanosis. Markedly decreased air entry bilaterally with diffuse crackles. No retractions visible due to severe muscle hypotonia. Pupils equal and sluggishly reactive. Fontanelle flat.
pmh: Born at 35 weeks gestation. No prior hospitalization. Up to date on vaccinations. No known medications.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 1: Requires immediate life-saving intervention. Apnea, SpO2 52%, GCS 8, agonal respirations — this patient requires immediate bag-mask ventilation and likely intubation. Delay of even one minute is unacceptable. Classic presentation of RSV bronchiolitis progressing to respiratory failure in a premature infant.
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies agonal breathing, SpO2 52%, and GCS 8 as ESI 1 criteria requiring immediate resuscitative intervention.
teaching_point: In infants <6 months, especially ex-premature, RSV can progress rapidly to apnea and respiratory failure. Any SpO2 <90% with altered mental status in this age group is ESI 1 until proven otherwise.

chat_responses.appearance: Limp infant with agonal gasping respirations.
chat_responses.respiratory: No retractions visible due to severe muscle hypotonia. Markedly decreased air entry bilaterally with diffuse crackles.
chat_responses.neuro: Pupils equal and sluggishly reactive. Fontanelle flat.

title: Anaphylaxis Post-Peanut Exposure
chief_complaint: Allergic reaction after eating peanut butter cookie
hpi: 4-year-old female presents via EMS 12 minutes after accidental ingestion of a peanut butter cookie at a birthday party. Known peanut allergy. Father administered one dose of diphenhydramine 12.5mg en route. EMS reports audible stridor and diffuse urticaria noted at scene. Blood pressure was 68/38 on scene. She is anxious, crying, and complaining of throat tightness.
exam: Diffuse urticaria on trunk, face, and extremities. Bilateral periorbital angioedema. Audible inspiratory stridor. Diffuse expiratory wheeze on auscultation. Intercostal retractions. Tongue appears mildly swollen. Hypotensive, tachycardic, tachypneic.
pmh: Known peanut and tree nut allergy diagnosed age 2. Prescribed EpiPen but not carried today. No prior anaphylaxis requiring ED visit. No asthma.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 1: Active anaphylaxis with hemodynamic compromise (hypotension, tachycardia), upper airway involvement (stridor, angioedema), and bronchospasm. Requires immediate IM epinephrine, IV access, fluid resuscitation, and airway monitoring. Delayed treatment carries mortality risk.
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies anaphylaxis with hypotension and stridor as ESI 1 — immediate life-saving intervention (epinephrine) required.
teaching_point: Diphenhydramine is NOT first-line for anaphylaxis and does not treat hemodynamic instability or upper airway edema. Epinephrine is the only effective immediate treatment. Antihistamines alone in the face of hemodynamic compromise represent a common dangerous error.

chat_responses.appearance: Diffuse urticaria on trunk, face, and extremities.
chat_responses.respiratory: Audible inspiratory stridor. Diffuse expiratory wheeze on auscultation. Intercostal retractions. Diffuse expiratory wheeze on auscultation.
chat_responses.neuro: No abnormal findings.
chat_responses.skin: Diffuse urticaria on trunk, face, and extremities. Bilateral periorbital angioedema.

She is anxious, crying, and complaining of throat tightness.

title: Submersion Injury — Unresponsive
hpi: 8-year-old male brought by EMS following submersion in a backyard pool. Found face-down by father, estimated time submerged 4–6 minutes. CPR initiated by bystander and continued by EMS en route. Arrived with return of spontaneous circulation approximately 3 minutes ago. Currently apneic with occasional gasping. EMS performed one round of CPR prior to arrival.
exam: Cyanotic, unresponsive to pain, pupils fixed and dilated at 5mm bilaterally. Gasping agonal respirations 4/min. No purposeful movement. Cool, mottled skin. Hypothermic (core temp 34.8°C). EMS BVM in progress on arrival.
pmh: No known medical history. No medications. Fully vaccinated.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 1: Post-cardiac arrest with ongoing agonal breathing, profound hemodynamic instability, and hypothermia. Requires immediate intubation, continued resuscitation, targeted temperature management, and ICU-level care. Every second without definitive airway and resuscitation worsens outcomes.
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies post-cardiac arrest with agonal breathing and GCS 3 as ESI 1.
teaching_point: In pediatric submersion, hypothermia may be protective — 'not dead until warm and dead.' Do not terminate resuscitation prematurely in hypothermic drowning victims. Continuous CPR during rewarming is standard. Immediate intubation and access are priority.

title: Toddler with Fever and Petechiae
hpi: 18-month-old female brought in by parents for 12 hours of fever up to 40.1°C at home and a rash that appeared 3 hours ago. Parents describe the rash as 'spots that don't go away when we press on them.' She has been fussier than usual and less interactive but still responsive. No vomiting. No diarrhea. No sick contacts that parents are aware of. Has not received antipyretics in 6 hours.
exam: Fussy but consolable. Non-blanching petechiae distributed across trunk and bilateral lower extremities, largest measuring 3mm. No purpuric plaques. Mildly decreased muscle tone. Mucous membranes moist. Anterior fontanelle flat. No nuchal rigidity (difficult to fully assess). No meningismus. Mild tachycardia and borderline tachypnea.
pmh: Born full-term. Up to date on vaccines. No prior hospitalizations. No medications.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 2: Non-blanching petechiae with fever in a toddler is meningococcemia until proven otherwise — a high-risk condition that requires immediate assessment by a physician. Even in the absence of frank hemodynamic instability, this presentation carries significant risk of rapid deterioration. Does not meet ESI 1 currently (no immediate life-saving intervention needed this second) but cannot wait. Needs IV access, blood cultures, CBC, LP consideration, and empiric antibiotics within minutes.
ai_rationale: AI INCORRECTLY assigns ESI 3. The AI interprets this as 'febrile child with rash requiring labs and IV' rather than recognizing the non-blanching character of the petechiae as a high-risk flag for meningococcemia. This is a dangerous undertriage error.
teaching_point: Non-blanching petechiae + fever = ESI 2 until meningococcemia is excluded. The absence of frank purpura, meningismus, or hemodynamic instability does NOT make this ESI 3. Early empiric ceftriaxone before culture results has been shown to improve outcomes. The AI's failure here illustrates a critical limitation: it may process vitals and surface features without applying the syndromic pattern recognition that an experienced clinician uses.

chat_responses.neuro: Anterior fontanelle flat. Mildly decreased muscle tone.

title: First-Time Seizure, Post-Ictal
hpi: 6-year-old male brought in by EMS after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately 3 minutes at home. No prior seizure history. No fever. No recent illness. No head trauma. No toxic ingestion suspected. Seizure resolved spontaneously before EMS arrival. Now in post-ictal state — opens eyes to voice, moaning, non-purposeful movement of extremities.
exam: Post-ictal. Eyes open to voice. Oriented to self only. Moaning but not following commands consistently. No focal neurological deficits appreciated. Pupils equal and reactive bilaterally. Afebrile. Slightly tachycardic. No rash. No signs of trauma.
pmh: No prior seizure disorder. No neurologic diagnoses. No medications. Born full-term, normal development.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 2: New-onset seizure with ongoing altered mental status (post-ictal, GCS 12). High-risk presentation requiring urgent physician evaluation — needs to rule out CNS infection, structural lesion, metabolic abnormality, toxic ingestion. Although currently hemodynamically stable, the altered mental status meets ESI 2 criteria. Should not sit in waiting room.
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies post-ictal state with GCS 12 and new-onset seizure as ESI 2 — altered mental status requiring prompt physician evaluation.
teaching_point: First-time unprovoked seizure in a school-age child warrants urgent workup (glucose, electrolytes, EEG, likely neuroimaging) even if vitals are stable. Post-ictal GCS depression is expected but should resolve within 30–60 minutes; failure to improve warrants urgent escalation.

chat_responses.neuro: No focal neurological deficits appreciated. Oriented to self only. Pupils equal and reactive bilaterally.

title: Adolescent with Suicidal Ideation and Plan
hpi: 14-year-old female brought to the ED by her mother after disclosing a suicide plan to a school counselor today. Patient reports passive suicidal ideation for the past 2 months but today told a friend she had collected her mother's sertraline (approximately 30 tablets) and planned to take them tonight after her parents fell asleep. She is alert, calm, and cooperative. Denies current intoxication.
exam: Alert and oriented x4. Appropriate affect, somewhat flat. Makes eye contact. No signs of acute intoxication or self-harm injury. No visible wounds. Physical exam unremarkable.
pmh: Diagnosed with major depressive disorder 6 months ago. Currently prescribed sertraline 50mg daily by PCP. No prior suicide attempts. No hospitalizations.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 2: High-risk suicidal ideation with specific lethal plan, means access (stockpiled medications), and disclosed timeline. Despite normal vitals and GCS 15, this is an ESI 2 — she cannot safely wait. Immediate physician evaluation is required for risk stratification, safety planning, and likely psychiatric disposition. The behavioral health triage criteria for ESI 2 include 'high-risk suicidal ideation.'
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies specific plan + means + timeline as ESI 2 behavioral health criteria requiring immediate physician evaluation.
teaching_point: ESI behavioral health triage: patients with suicidal ideation WITH a specific plan, means, or recent attempt are ESI 2 regardless of normal physiologic vitals. Normal GCS and stable vitals are expected in this population — do not be falsely reassured. Medication access must be addressed urgently.

title: Toddler with Altered Mental Status — Possible Ingestion
chief_complaint: Found sleepy and hard to wake up
hpi: 2-year-old male brought in by grandmother who found him 'very sleepy and hard to wake up' approximately 45 minutes ago. He had been playing unsupervised in the living room for 20 minutes. Grandmother takes oxycodone 5mg/acetaminophen for chronic back pain and is unsure if her pill organizer is fully accounted for. He vomited once at home. No fever. No known prior illness.
exam: Drowsy but rousable to sternal rub. GCS 11 (E3V3M5). Pinpoint pupils bilaterally (2mm), non-reactive to light. Decreased muscle tone. Shallow respirations. No obvious trauma. Moist mucous membranes. Normal skin turgor. Bowel sounds present.
pmh: No significant past medical history. No medications. No prior ingestions.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 2: Opioid toxidrome in a toddler — miosis, altered mental status, decreased respiratory rate, and bradycardia in a child with access to opioids. GCS 11 with a depressed respiratory drive in a 2-year-old is immediately dangerous. Naloxone may be required at any moment. This child can deteriorate to apnea within minutes. Requires immediate physician assessment, pulse oximetry monitoring, and likely naloxone administration.
ai_rationale: AI INCORRECTLY assigns ESI 3. The AI notes altered mental status and possible ingestion but assigns resources (labs, IV) without recognizing the full opioid toxidrome pattern (miosis + bradycardia + decreased RR + AMS) that constitutes an immediately high-risk presentation requiring urgent intervention.
teaching_point: Pinpoint pupils + bradycardia + decreased RR + AMS = opioid toxidrome. This is ESI 2 regardless of current SpO2. Opioid-mediated respiratory depression can deteriorate rapidly in toddlers given their high metabolic rate and relatively smaller respiratory reserve. The mnemonic 'SLUDGE' applies to cholinergic toxidrome; for opioids, remember the triad: miosis, CNS depression, respiratory depression.

chat_responses.appearance: Drowsy but rousable to sternal rub.
chat_responses.neuro: GCS 11 (E3V3M5). Pinpoint pupils bilaterally (2mm), non-reactive to light. Decreased muscle tone.

title: Severe Acute Asthma — Speaking in Words
hpi: 10-year-old female with known severe persistent asthma presents with acute exacerbation over the past 4 hours. She received 3 doses of albuterol MDI via spacer at home with minimal improvement. She is speaking in single words and phrases. Reports significant chest tightness. She has been hospitalized twice in the past year, including one PICU admission.
exam: Anxious, sitting upright, tripoding. Speaking in 1–2 word phrases. Audible wheeze without stethoscope. Severe intercostal and suprasternal retractions. Prolonged expiratory phase. Decreased air entry at bilateral bases. Trachea midline. No pulsus paradoxus measured yet. Accessory muscle use prominent.
pmh: Severe persistent asthma. On fluticasone/salmeterol combination inhaler, montelukast, and rescue albuterol. Two prior hospitalizations, one PICU admission for respiratory failure. History of intubation at age 7.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 2: Severe asthma with SpO2 88%, speaking in words only, severe retractions, and inadequate response to beta-agonists at home. This patient has risk factors for near-fatal asthma (prior intubation, PICU admission). Requires immediate continuous albuterol nebulization, IV magnesium sulfate consideration, IV corticosteroids, and physician at bedside. Cannot wait.
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies SpO2 88%, single-word speech, severe retractions, and prior PICU history as ESI 2 — high-risk, urgent physician evaluation required.
teaching_point: SpO2 <90% in asthma is a late and ominous sign of gas trapping and impending respiratory failure. Prior PICU admission or intubation is the single strongest predictor of near-fatal asthma — these patients must be treated as ESI 2 regardless of current appearance. Silent chest (no wheeze) = worse than wheeze.

chat_responses.respiratory: Audible wheeze without stethoscope. Severe intercostal and suprasternal retractions.

title: RLQ Pain — Possible Appendicitis
hpi: 7-year-old male presents with 18 hours of worsening right lower quadrant abdominal pain. Pain began periumbilically and migrated to the RLQ over 12 hours. Associated anorexia — refused dinner and breakfast. One episode of vomiting this morning. Low-grade fever. Pain is constant, 6/10 on faces scale, and worsened by movement. He walked into the ED bent slightly forward.
exam: Ill-appearing but alert. Guarding and point tenderness at McBurney's point. Positive Rovsing's sign. Psoas sign positive. No rigidity or peritoneal signs at this time. Bowel sounds diminished. Voluntary guarding on palpation.
pmh: No prior abdominal surgeries. No known GI diagnoses. Healthy child, no medications.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 3: Classic appendicitis presentation requiring multiple resources — IV access, CBC with differential, CMP, CRP, urinalysis, abdominal ultrasound ± CT, surgical consultation, IV fluids, IV analgesia. The presentation is concerning enough that this is not a 'one-resource' visit. Needs prompt evaluation given risk of perforation.
ai_rationale: AI INCORRECTLY assigns ESI 4. The AI identifies 'abdominal pain needing evaluation' but fails to recognize the migration of pain, associated fever, anorexia, positive peritoneal signs pattern as a multi-resource surgical workup. This underestimates resource needs and urgency.
teaching_point: Classic Migratory RLQ pain + anorexia + fever in a school-age child = appendicitis until proven otherwise and warrants ESI 3. The Pediatric Appendicitis Score (PAS) and Alvarado score both rely on this symptom cluster. ESI is about resource prediction — appendicitis workup routinely requires ≥2 resources (labs + imaging + surgical consult). ESI 4 would imply only one resource is needed.

title: Febrile Neonate — 5 Weeks Old
hpi: 5-week-old female brought in by parents for rectal temperature of 38.2°C measured at home 2 hours ago. This is her first febrile illness. She was born at 39 weeks, uncomplicated delivery. Feeding well — breastfed, normal wet diapers, normal stools. Fussier than usual today. No sick contacts known. No rash. No vomiting.
exam: Alert, fussy but consolable. Good tone and color. Anterior fontanelle flat and soft. No bulging. Mucous membranes moist. No rash. No respiratory distress. Tachycardic for age. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no hepatosplenomegaly.
pmh: Born at 39 weeks, uncomplicated vaginal delivery. Passed newborn screen. No antibiotics since birth. No prior ED visits.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 2: Any infant <28 days with fever ≥38.0°C is automatically ESI 2 per most pediatric triage protocols. At 5 weeks, this falls into the 29–60 day category where serious bacterial infection risk remains significant (~10%). Standard febrile neonate workup (full sepsis evaluation: CBC, CMP, blood culture, UA/urine culture, LP) and empiric antibiotics are standard of care per Rochester, Boston, and Philadelphia criteria. Cannot wait in the waiting room.
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies a febrile 5-week-old as ESI 2 — this age group has protocol-level triage elevation regardless of appearance.
teaching_point: Febrile neonates 0–28 days are ESI 1 or 2. At 29–60 days, most protocols treat as ESI 2 with full workup. The 'well-appearing' qualifier is unreliable in this age group — neonates with bacteremia or meningitis can appear deceptively well early in their course. Clinical gestalt should NOT be used to downgrade from ESI 2 in this cohort.

title: Wrist Fracture After Fall
chief_complaint: Wrist pain after fall off skateboard
hpi: 12-year-old male presents with right wrist pain after falling off a skateboard approximately 1 hour ago. He reports pain 7/10, worsened with any movement. Fell onto an outstretched hand. Visible deformity at the distal radius. He is able to wiggle his fingers but reports some numbness in the distribution of his thumb and index finger. No other injuries identified.
exam: Obvious dorsal angulation at distal right radius with mild swelling and ecchymosis. Tenderness over distal radius and anatomical snuffbox. Radial pulse 2+ and intact. Sensation decreased to light touch over first webspace bilaterally. Flexor and extensor strength 4/5 limited by pain. No crepitus on exam. No open wound.
pmh: No prior fractures. No bleeding disorders. No medications. Active and healthy.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 3: Multiple resources needed — X-ray (at minimum wrist, possibly scaphoid views given snuffbox tenderness), IV access for pain management (likely requires procedural sedation for reduction), orthopedic consultation. Neurovascular compromise (decreased sensation in median/radial distribution) adds urgency. Not ESI 4 (one resource) given the complexity and likely need for reduction.
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies obvious fracture with neurovascular symptoms requiring imaging, IV analgesia, and possible procedural sedation as ESI 3.
teaching_point: Key teaching: anatomical snuffbox tenderness raises concern for scaphoid fracture which may not appear on initial X-ray. Decreased sensation in first webspace suggests median or anterior interosseous nerve injury — neurovascular status should be documented and re-evaluated after reduction and splinting. ESI 3 given multi-resource need.

title: Mild-Moderate Asthma Exacerbation
chief_complaint: Asthma flare — wheeze and shortness of breath
hpi: 9-year-old male with moderate persistent asthma presents with 6 hours of increasing wheeze and shortness of breath following URI symptoms for 2 days. He received two doses of albuterol MDI at home with partial response. He is speaking in full sentences, mildly anxious. No previous ICU admission. One hospitalization 18 months ago for asthma.
exam: Mild-to-moderate respiratory distress. Speaking in full sentences. Mild intercostal retractions. Bilateral expiratory wheeze on auscultation, good air entry. No use of accessory muscles. No nasal flaring.
pmh: Moderate persistent asthma. On budesonide inhaler and albuterol PRN. One prior hospitalization, no PICU admissions. Spring/fall seasonal exacerbations typical.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 3: Multiple resources needed — albuterol nebulization (likely multiple treatments), oral or IV corticosteroids, pulse oximetry monitoring, possible chest X-ray. SpO2 95% with partial response to home therapy and ongoing retractions warrants ESI 3 rather than ESI 4, which would imply a single-resource visit.
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies mild-moderate asthma with SpO2 95%, partial beta-agonist response, and ongoing retractions as ESI 3.
teaching_point: Contrast this case with Case 8 (ESI 2): SpO2 95% vs 88%, full sentences vs single words, prior hospitalization only vs PICU/intubation history. These distinctions drive the ESI 2 vs 3 differentiation. ESI 3 asthma still requires multiple resources and prompt treatment but allows brief wait if resources are being gathered.

title: Adolescent with Palpitations and Chest Pain
chief_complaint: Palpitations and chest pain for 2 hours
hpi: 15-year-old female presents with 2 hours of sudden-onset palpitations and chest tightness. Onset was abrupt while sitting at rest watching TV. Denies syncope, pre-syncope, or dizziness. Mild shortness of breath. No fever or illness. First episode of this type. No family history of sudden cardiac death. No prior cardiac workup. No medications or supplement use. Denies caffeine, energy drinks, or illicit drug use.
exam: Alert and anxious. Regular rapid rhythm — no pulse deficit. Neck veins slightly distended. Frog sign noted (visible neck pulsations). Lungs clear. No murmur audible but cardiac auscultation limited by rapid rate. Mild pallor. Moist mucous membranes.
pmh: No prior cardiac history. No family history of arrhythmia or sudden death. Not on any medications. No prior ECG.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 3: HR 214 with borderline hypotension (BP 94/58), 2-hour duration, and no prior cardiac history. This is almost certainly supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) and requires ECG, IV access, rhythm monitoring, vagal maneuvers or adenosine. Multiple resources needed. Although currently compensated, the borderline hypotension and HR 214 preclude this being a simple 'one-resource' ESI 4 visit.
ai_rationale: AI INCORRECTLY assigns ESI 4. The AI acknowledges tachycardia and chest pain but interprets this as 'palpitations needing ECG only,' missing the borderline hypotension (94/58) and HR 214 that together suggest a potentially hemodynamically significant arrhythmia requiring more than one resource.
teaching_point: HR 214 with any hemodynamic compromise in a 15-year-old is not a single-resource visit. SVT at this rate with borderline hypotension requires IV access, monitoring, vagal maneuvers with physician at bedside, and probable adenosine administration — this is ESI 3, not ESI 4. The combination of HR >200 + hypotension = potential for rapid decompensation.

title: Croup with Stridor at Rest
hpi: 3-year-old male with 2-day history of barky, seal-like cough and low-grade fever. Tonight developed audible inspiratory stridor at rest. Parents report he woke up from sleep with the noisy breathing. No drooling. No dysphagia. No recent choking events. No ill contacts with epiglottitis. Has not received nebulized racemic epinephrine previously.
exam: Alert, mildly anxious, sitting upright in parent's lap. Audible inspiratory stridor at rest (not only with crying). Mild intercostal retractions. Good air entry bilaterally. No drooling. Neck supple. No lymphadenopathy. Moderate barky cough during exam. Oropharynx normal without erythema, no exudates.
pmh: No prior croup episodes. No prior intubation or stridor. No hospitalizations. No medications. No known anatomic airway abnormalities.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 3: Stridor at rest represents moderate-severe croup (Westley score ≥3) and requires multiple resources — nebulized racemic epinephrine, dexamethasone (oral or IM), pulse oximetry monitoring, and extended observation period (minimum 2 hours post-epinephrine to assess for rebound). This is NOT a one-resource visit. Stridor at REST (vs only with agitation) is the critical distinction.
ai_rationale: AI INCORRECTLY assigns ESI 4. The AI identifies 'croup needing dexamethasone' as a single-resource visit, missing the clinical significance of stridor at rest vs only with crying/agitation — a key distinction that elevates this to moderate-severe croup requiring multiple interventions.
teaching_point: Stridor ONLY with crying/agitation = mild croup, potentially ESI 4. Stridor AT REST = moderate-severe croup, ESI 3. This distinction is critical for triage. Moderate-severe croup requires: (1) dexamethasone, (2) racemic epinephrine, (3) SpO2 monitoring, (4) ≥2h observation post-epi. That is definitionally a multi-resource visit. The AI's error here reflects pattern matching on 'croup' as a benign entity without integrating the rest severity modifier.

title: Ankle Sprain — Able to Bear Weight Partially
hpi: 8-year-old female twisted her right ankle during soccer practice 2 hours ago. She was able to walk off the field with assistance. Reports pain 4/10, localized to the lateral ankle. No other injuries. She has been able to take a few steps with a notable limp. Ibuprofen 200mg given at home with mild relief.
exam: Alert, well-appearing, in mild distress. Lateral ankle swelling with mild ecchymosis. Tenderness over the anterior talofibular ligament. Negative Ottawa Ankle Rules (no bony tenderness at posterior tip or base of fibula or posterior tip of tibia). Able to weight-bear with a limp. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses intact. Sensation intact. Capillary refill brisk.
pmh: No prior ankle injuries. No bleeding disorders. Healthy, active child.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 4: Negative Ottawa Ankle Rules in an 8-year-old (though Ottawa rules are less validated <18) with partial weight-bearing, lateral tenderness, and a mechanism consistent with lateral ligament sprain. One resource needed: X-ray to evaluate for growth plate fracture given skeletal immaturity. Can be safely triaged as ESI 4 with pain management and X-ray.
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies partial weight-bearing ankle injury with negative Ottawa equivalent criteria as ESI 4 requiring X-ray only.
teaching_point: Ottawa Ankle Rules have reduced sensitivity in children due to growth plate vulnerability. Any pediatric ankle injury with bony tenderness should have imaging even with negative Ottawa criteria. However, this case is lateral ligament soft tissue injury — appropriate for ESI 4. Pain 4/10, partial weight-bearing, negative bony tenderness on exam = one resource (X-ray).

title: Cellulitis — Leg, Afebrile
hpi: 6-year-old male with 2-day history of increasing redness and swelling on his right lower leg. Mother noticed it after he scraped his knee at the playground 4 days ago. Area of erythema has expanded — she marked the border with a pen 8 hours ago and the redness has spread approximately 2cm beyond her mark. No fever at home. No drainage from wound. No other systemic symptoms.
exam: Alert, well-appearing, comfortable. 6x8cm area of erythema, warmth, and induration on anterior right lower leg. Non-fluctuant. No streaking (lymphangitis). Small dried abrasion at center of erythema. No lymphadenopathy. Wound margins well-defined. Afebrile in ED.
pmh: No prior skin infections. No diabetes or immunodeficiency. No prior MRSA. No recent antibiotics.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 4: Afebrile, non-fluctuant, non-complicated cellulitis with clear trigger (abrasion) and well-demarcated borders. One resource: wound culture if clinical judgment dictates, plus prescription for antibiotics (cephalexin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for MRSA coverage if indicated). No IV needed, no imaging needed.
ai_rationale: AI flags the 2cm border progression in 8 hours as a high-risk feature and recommends ESI 3, citing concern for rapidly spreading cellulitis with possible bacteremia requiring IV access, CBC, and blood cultures. AI interprets spreading erythema as an early warning sign of sepsis in a pediatric patient.
teaching_point: AI overtriage: Spreading cellulitis does warrant monitoring, but this child is afebrile, hemodynamically normal, and well-appearing — classic ESI 4. ESI 3 would require a high-risk feature such as fever, systemic toxicity, concern for necrotizing fasciitis, or immunocompromise. Marking borders at triage is good practice, but 2cm spread over 8 hours in an afebrile child without systemic signs does not justify IV access or bloodwork. Teaching opportunity: recognize when AI pattern-matches to a dangerous diagnosis (spreading infection = sepsis) without integrating the full clinical picture.

title: Adolescent with UTI Symptoms
hpi: 16-year-old female presents with 2 days of dysuria, urinary frequency, and suprapubic discomfort. No fever. No back or flank pain. No vaginal discharge or odor. Sexually active, last menstrual period 10 days ago. Denies pregnancy symptoms. No hematuria visible. First UTI episode. No prior antibiotic use recently.
exam: Alert, well-appearing, no acute distress. Mild suprapubic tenderness on palpation. No costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally. No vaginal discharge visible on visual inspection. Abdomen soft. No guarding or rebound.
pmh: No prior UTIs. No gynecologic diagnoses. No medications. Sexually active, no contraception currently.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 4: Classic uncomplicated lower UTI presentation in an adolescent. One resource needed: urinalysis with reflex culture. Additional consideration (not resource-intensive): urine pregnancy test given sexually active status and potential for STI co-evaluation, but primary resource is UA. Outpatient antibiotics can follow. Afebrile with no flank tenderness argues against pyelonephritis.
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies uncomplicated cystitis symptoms in an afebrile, otherwise well adolescent as ESI 4.
teaching_point: Always obtain urine pregnancy test in sexually active adolescent females presenting with pelvic/abdominal symptoms regardless of LMP date. Ectopic pregnancy can present with dysuria. No CVA tenderness + no fever + lower urinary symptoms = lower UTI. Fever + CVA tenderness = pyelonephritis = ESI 3 (IV access, IV antibiotics, possible admission).

title: Ear Pain with Low-Grade Fever
chief_complaint: Right ear pain and hearing muffled
hpi: 11-year-old male with 3 days of progressive right-sided ear pain and muffled hearing in that ear. Low-grade fever today at home (37.9°C). Mild URI symptoms preceding ear pain by 5 days. No ear drainage. No vertigo. No facial droop. No mastoid tenderness. Ibuprofen has given partial relief.
exam: Alert, well-appearing, mild distress due to pain. Right tympanic membrane erythematous, opaque, and bulging with loss of normal landmarks. No perforation. Light reflex absent. No post-auricular tenderness. Facial nerve intact. Left ear normal. Oropharynx mildly erythematous, no exudates.
pmh: One prior AOM episode 2 years ago. Not on prophylactic antibiotics. No prior ear surgery. No hearing aids. No recent dental work.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 4: Acute otitis media — bulging TM, opaque, loss of landmarks, with appropriate fever and antecedent URI. One resource needed: physical examination (already being done by clinician) and prescription. No labs, no imaging needed. Low-grade fever in context of AOM is expected and not a reason to escalate.
ai_rationale: AI recommends ESI 3, citing fever (38.0°C), muffled hearing, and 3-day progression as features concerning for complicated AOM or early mastoiditis requiring imaging (temporal bone CT), IV antibiotics, and otolaryngology consult. AI interprets loss of light reflex and opaque TM as high-risk findings.
teaching_point: AI overtriage: Mastoiditis is a genuine AOM complication but requires post-auricular tenderness, swelling, or proptosis of the pinna — none of which are present here. This is straightforward AOM with expected low-grade fever. ESI 3 would require high resource use (CT, IV, consult) which is not indicated without mastoid findings. Teaching opportunity: fever in the context of an obvious focal infection does not automatically escalate ESI. Treat the infection causing the fever, not the number on the thermometer.

title: Superficial Abrasion — Well-Appearing Child
chief_complaint: Scraped knee after falling off bicycle
hpi: 5-year-old male fell off his bicycle while riding without a helmet on a gravel path approximately 30 minutes ago. Reports pain 2/10 at the right knee. No LOC. No vomiting. Acting normally. Scraped knee was cleaned with water by parent at home. He walked into the ED and is asking for a sticker.
exam: Alert, interactive, running around the triage area. 4x3cm superficial gravel abrasion on right knee, actively bleeding stopped, no deep tissue involvement, no foreign body visible. No bony tenderness. Full range of motion at knee. No other injuries identified. No scalp lac, no facial injuries. Head CT not indicated.
pmh: Healthy. Fully vaccinated — tetanus up to date (DTaP #4 given at age 4). No medications. No bleeding disorders.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 5: Minor injury requiring no diagnostic resources. Wound cleaning and bandaging is nursing-level care with no physician resources required. Tetanus up to date. Hemostasis achieved. This child will be seen, cleaned, bandaged, and discharged with wound care instructions — zero resources (labs, imaging, medications, IV, consult) required.
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies superficial abrasion requiring no resources as ESI 5.
teaching_point: ESI 5 is the 'no resource' category. Common ED visits that meet ESI 5: superficial lacerations, minor abrasions, medication refills, prescription requests, normal newborn concerns, and minor rashes. These patients can safely wait. Over-triaging ESI 5 patients to ESI 4 wastes resources and increases wait times for acutely ill patients.

title: Medication Refill — Stable ADHD
chief_complaint: Needs refill for ADHD medication, ran out 2 days ago
hpi: 13-year-old female presents requesting a refill of her methylphenidate extended-release 27mg daily. She ran out 2 days ago and her prescribing psychiatrist is on vacation. No acute symptoms. No psychiatric crisis. No suicidal ideation. Parents report she has been 'a bit more distracted and impulsive' since running out but no safety concerns. She is scheduled to see her psychiatrist in 10 days.
exam: Alert, pleasant, talkative. Normal neurological exam. No signs of withdrawal or acute psychiatric decompensation. No dysphoria or agitation. Vitals unremarkable. No tachycardia or hypertension.
pmh: ADHD, combined presentation, diagnosed age 8. Current medication: methylphenidate ER 27mg (Concerta) daily. No other diagnoses. No prior psychiatric hospitalization. No substance use history.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 5: Administrative/prescription visit with no acute medical problem. Methylphenidate is a Schedule II controlled substance — bridging prescriptions from the ED is a complex prescribing issue but from a triage perspective, this patient has no acute complaint, no physiologic instability, and requires no diagnostic resources. Zero resources needed.
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies a prescription refill request with no acute symptoms as ESI 5 — no resources required.
teaching_point: ESI 5 includes administrative and prescription-related visits without acute symptoms. From a triage standpoint, this is ESI 5 regardless of the complexity of the prescribing issue (that is a clinical and legal question for the treating provider). Important safety caveat: always screen ESI 5 behavioral health visits for safety, as 'routine' presentations can mask acute psychiatric needs.
